description: A comprehensive guide to learning the C programming language from basics to system-level concepts.

topics.title: Variables & Data Types
topics.definition: Variables are named memory locations used to store data that can be changed during program execution. Each variable in C must be explicitly declared with a specific data type before it can be used.
topics.explanation: C is a statically typed language, meaning the compiler must know the type of every variable. This allows the compiler to allocate the exact amount of memory needed and perform type-checking to prevent errors.
pros: Memory efficiency: You allocate exactly what you need.
Performance: Static typing allows for faster execution.
Direct hardware control: Essential for embedded systems.
cons: Strict rules: Forgetting to declare a variable causes compilation errors.
Manual management: No automatic garbage collection.
Pointer complexity: Advanced types require understanding memory addresses.

examples.title: Basic Declarations

bestPractices: Always initialize variables before using them to prevent garbage values.
Use meaningful, descriptive variable names (e.g., `total_score` instead of `ts`).
Use `const` for variables whose values should never change.
commonMistakes: Using a variable before declaring it.
Assigning a float value to an int variable without realizing it truncates the decimal.
Forgetting the semicolon `;` at the end of the declaration.

resources.title: The C Programming Language (2nd Edition)
resources.author: Brian W. Kernighan & Dennis M. Ritchie
